_meta.notes: Five regional festivals — one per spoke. Date triggers fire on UTC date matches; the world calendar (lib/story/calendar) consults this file to inject festival prompts into runs that take place in the relevant region during the festival window. Phase-9 expansion.

festivals.summary: the first morning the cloudtea bushes wake with frost on their leaves. the Whitebark families bring out their last-cellar cider; the Topgroves taste-test next year's plantings.
festivals.narratorPromptFragment: It is First Frost in Highfield. The cloudtea is freshly bitten by overnight cold; cider is poured at every terrace. Local NPCs are kinder than usual to outsiders today. Weave the festival into ambient detail.
ambient: A Whitebark family pours a year-old cider at the terrace edge.
The frost on a single cloudtea leaf catches the morning light.
A Gust-watcher pauses their measurement to taste.

festivals.summary: one day a year all elven contracts are publicly read in the Pontoon Mile. The act drops, briefly. After sundown it goes back up, unchanged.
festivals.narratorPromptFragment: It is The Audit in Saltgale. Elven NPCs today drop the act and speak in their off-act voice — terse, profane, kind only after they decide. Contracts are read aloud in the Pontoon Mile.
ambient: A Branchman reads a contract aloud in the open street; a competitor quietly takes notes.
A Vellum hands a sealed document to a clerk and walks away without it.
Salt-folk wholesalers laugh too loud — they are enjoying this.

festivals.summary: the only day Hush-readers may speak. They don't. The other orcs honor the silence by writing more than usual.
festivals.narratorPromptFragment: It is Marginalia Day in the Long Indices. The Hush-readers' silence is voluntary today, which makes it heavier. Ambient prose should foreground the writing — hands pause over folios, marginalia accrue across the room.
ambient: A Hush-reader holds a quill and does not put it to paper for a long time.
A Cataloguer's slip-pile grows by one for every hour without a word.
Three Marginals meet around a single folio and write across each other.

festivals.summary: each new master names their first solo bolt of cloth. The names are inscribed in the loom-square's cloth-name register, never to repeat.
festivals.narratorPromptFragment: It is Naming Day in Threadwarden. New masters are naming their first bolts; the loom-square is unusually crowded; the apprentices who will not become masters this year cry openly without being noticed.
ambient: A new master writes 'Late October' into the cloth-name register and does not look up.
An apprentice whose name will not be added this year ties a thread very carefully.
Old masters watch from the colonnade and remember the names of their own bolts.

festivals.summary: by old halfling tradition, a tide that comes when the moon does not pull it. Drowncallers stand watch; nothing usually happens; the watch is the festival.
festivals.narratorPromptFragment: It is Tide Without Cause at the Coral Anchorage. Drowncallers stand watch at the reef-edge. No fishing today; no contracts signed. The dawn-meal is doubled; afterward, only watching.
ambient: A Drowncaller's salt-cured face is angled at the horizon and has not moved in an hour.
The dawn-meal galley is unusually full and unusually quiet.
A child of the Anchorage attends their first watch and tries not to fidget.
